teaching_arc.entry_point: Start from the practical goal of building a reliable development machine.
teaching_arc.progression: Walk through preparation, installation, and verification with clear caution points.
teaching_arc.destination: Students should finish able to execute the install carefully or identify where they need help before proceeding.

audience_level: introductory autonomous mobile robotics students learning ROS 2 through lab-first workflows
topic_goal: Help students install Ubuntu safely and understand why the Linux environment matters for the rest of the ROS 2 workflow.
topic_takeaways: Students should know the key preparation and risk points of dual-boot installation.
Students should understand why a clean Ubuntu environment helps later robotics tooling.
Students should know the checkpoints to verify before and after installation.
style_notes: Keep the tone hands-on and confidence-building.
Connect every concept to what students will debug in a terminal, simulation, or robot lab.
Use visualization and mental models before deep command details.
Keep the pacing calm and careful.
Treat risky steps explicitly and avoid rushing past verification.
transition_style: Move from preparation to installation to post-install checks, always stating what must be verified before the next step.

slides.narration_seed: Follow the steps exactly. Stop and ask if anything looks different.
slides.transition_from_previous: Open by framing the topic goal: Help students install Ubuntu safely and understand why the Linux environment matters for the rest of the ROS 2 workflow.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into Ubuntu install options (3 choices) so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
slides.explanation_style: Frame Ubuntu 22.04 Dual Boot (Windows) as the question or capability the rest of the topic is going to answer.
slides.story_hint: Keep Ubuntu 22.04 Dual Boot (Windows) connected to the lab workflow students will use later in ROS 2 and mobile robotics.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should understand how Ubuntu 22.04 Dual Boot (Windows) sets up the lesson and why the topic matters.

slides.narration_seed: Keep Ubuntu install options (3 choices) efficient and procedural, highlighting only the information students need to act or debug correctly. Keep the pacing efficient and procedural rather than reading the slide word for word.
must_cover: Dual boot: Windows + Ubuntu on the same machine (best performance for sim)
Virtual Machine (VM): Ubuntu inside Windows/macOS (lowest risk, but slower for Gazebo)
Replace OS: Ubuntu only (only if you’re sure you want it)
slides.transition_from_previous: After the concept slide, move into the concrete workflow or command sequence students will actually run.
slides.transition_to_next: After the procedure, remind students what successful output or behavior they should expect next.
likely_student_confusion: Students may copy steps mechanically and miss the verification point that tells them the step actually worked.
slides.explanation_style: Keep Ubuntu install options (3 choices) efficient and procedural, highlighting only the information students need to act or debug correctly.
slides.story_hint: Frame the slide as a lab workflow students will run themselves, including what they should verify before moving on.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should know the minimum reliable workflow on Ubuntu install options (3 choices) and what to verify before moving on.

slide_ref.source_title: What we do in class (and why)

slides.narration_seed: Use What we do in class (and why) to preview the route of the lesson, explain how this section fits the overall teaching arc, and tell students what to listen for next.
must_cover: In class we focus on dual boot because ROS + Gazebo runs better
VM is OK for learning commands, but can be slow for simulation
Dual boot is reversible: Ubuntu can be removed later (general idea)
slides.transition_from_previous: Bridge from Ubuntu install options (3 choices) into What we do in class (and why) so the lesson feels like one continuous explanation rather than isolated slides.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into If you want Windows-only later (general idea) so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
slides.explanation_style: Use What we do in class (and why) to orient students, preview the route, and tell them what to listen for next.
slides.story_hint: Keep What we do in class (and why) connected to the lab workflow students will use later in ROS 2 and mobile robotics.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should understand how What we do in class (and why) sets up the lesson and why the topic matters.

slide_ref.source_title: If you want Windows-only later (general idea)

slides.narration_seed: Define If you want Windows-only later (general idea) in plain language, then connect the term to one practical robotics consequence so students do not memorize it in isolation.
must_cover: Ubuntu is installed in its own partitions (in the free space you created)
To remove Ubuntu later: boot Windows → delete Ubuntu partitions → extend Windows partition
Then set Windows Boot Manager as first boot option (BIOS/UEFI)
If the boot menu remains, we handle it as a class/support step (don’t panic)
slides.transition_from_previous: Use If you want Windows-only later (general idea) to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once If you want Windows-only later (general idea) is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.
slides.explanation_style: Define If you want Windows-only later (general idea) in plain technical language, then attach one practical robotics consequence or example.
slides.story_hint: Keep If you want Windows-only later (general idea) connected to the lab workflow students will use later in ROS 2 and mobile robotics.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to explain If you want Windows-only later (general idea) accurately and use it in the next step of the lesson.

slides.narration_seed: Use Before we start (checklist) as a final self-check so students can tell whether they now understand the core ideas of the topic.
must_cover: Laptop plugged in (charger connected)
USB flash drive 8 GB+ (empty if possible)
Download Ubuntu 22.04 LTS Desktop ISO
Students should know the key preparation and risk points of dual-boot installation.
Download Rufus (Windows) to make bootable USB
slides.transition_from_previous: Use Before we start (checklist) to consolidate what the last few slides have built up and turn it into memory.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into Windows: turn off Fast Startup (recommended) so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
slides.explanation_style: Use Before we start (checklist) as a final self-check so students leave knowing what they should now be able to explain or do.
slides.story_hint: Keep Before we start (checklist) connected to the lab workflow students will use later in ROS 2 and mobile robotics.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should leave Before we start (checklist) with the main takeaways of the topic compressed into usable memory.

must_cover: Open Control Panel → Power Options
Click Choose what the power buttons do
Click Change settings that are currently unavailable
Uncheck Turn on fast startup → Save changes

slide_ref.source_title: Windows: create Unallocated space (do this carefully)

slides.narration_seed: Keep Windows: create Unallocated space (do this carefully) efficient and procedural, highlighting only the information students need to act or debug correctly. Keep the pacing efficient and procedural rather than reading the slide word for word.
must_cover: Right-click (C:) → choose Shrink Volume…
Enter how much to shrink (suggestion: 60000–120000 MB = 60–120 GB)
Click Shrink
Confirm you now see Unallocated space (black bar) on the disk

slides.narration_seed: Define Rufus: choose partition scheme (important) in plain language, then connect the term to one practical robotics consequence so students do not memorize it in isolation.
must_cover: If your PC uses UEFI (most modern laptops): choose GPT
If your PC is older and uses Legacy BIOS: choose MBR
If you are unsure: choose GPT (most students will use GPT/UEFI)
Click START → confirm erase warning
slides.transition_from_previous: Use Rufus: choose partition scheme (important) to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.

Open Boot Menu key during startup (common keys: F12 / ESC / F9 / F10)
Choose the USB device (often shows as UEFI: )
You should see the Ubuntu boot screen

slide_ref.source_title: Installer: Installation type (DO NOT pick the wrong one)

slides.narration_seed: Turn Installer: Installation type (DO NOT pick the wrong one) into a short guided practice moment: let students think first, then explain the reasoning in a teacher-like way.
must_cover: If you see Install Ubuntu alongside Windows Boot Manager → you can select it (easy option)
If you want full control (recommended in class): choose Something else
Click Continue
Give students a moment to decide before explaining the answer.
slides.transition_from_previous: Turn the previous explanation into a decision on Installer: Installation type (DO NOT pick the wrong one) and let students test whether they can reason with the idea.
slides.transition_to_next: Use the next slide to generalize the reasoning students just practiced.
likely_student_confusion: Students may rush to the answer on Installer: Installation type (DO NOT pick the wrong one) without first stating the reasoning steps.
slides.explanation_style: Turn Installer: Installation type (DO NOT pick the wrong one) into a guided decision: pause for thought, then reveal the reasoning step by step.
slides.story_hint: Frame the slide as a lab workflow students will run themselves, including what they should verify before moving on.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to answer a similar Installer: Installation type (DO NOT pick the wrong one) question on their own and explain the reasoning.

must_cover: In the partition list, look for Free space (Unallocated)
Make sure you are NOT selecting Windows partitions
We will create Ubuntu partitions ONLY inside the free space

slides.narration_seed: Keep Create the Ubuntu root partition ( / ) efficient and procedural, highlighting only the information students need to act or debug correctly. Keep the pacing efficient and procedural rather than reading the slide word for word.
must_cover: Select Free space → click +
Size: at least 25 GB (recommend 40–60 GB if possible)
Type: Primary
Location: Beginning of this space

slides.narration_seed: Keep Create /home partition (recommended) efficient and procedural, highlighting only the information students need to act or debug correctly. Keep the pacing efficient and procedural rather than reading the slide word for word.
must_cover: Select remaining Free space → click +
Size: use most of the remaining space (leave swap if you want swap)
Use as: Ext4 journaling file system
Mount point: /home
slides.transition_from_previous: After the concept slide, move into the concrete workflow or command sequence students will actually run.

slides.narration_seed: Define Swap area (optional) in plain language, then connect the term to one practical robotics consequence so students do not memorize it in isolation.
must_cover: Swap is extra memory on disk when RAM is full (slower than RAM)
If you want swap: select remaining free space → +
Use as: swap area
Common size: 2–8 GB (or similar to RAM if you want hibernation)
slides.transition_from_previous: Use Swap area (optional) to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.

slide_ref.source_title: EFI partition (do NOT create a new one if Windows already has it)

slides.narration_seed: Keep EFI partition (do NOT create a new one if Windows already has it) efficient and procedural, highlighting only the information students need to act or debug correctly. Keep the pacing efficient and procedural rather than reading the slide word for word.
must_cover: Most Windows UEFI systems already have an EFI System Partition (ESP)
If you see a small FAT32 partition labeled EFI/ESP → keep it (do not format it)
Ubuntu will use the existing EFI partition automatically
Only create EFI if the installer warns you and your disk truly has none
slides.transition_from_previous: After the concept slide, move into the concrete workflow or command sequence students will actually run.
slides.transition_to_next: After the procedure, remind students what successful output or behavior they should expect next.

slides.delivery_goal: Students should know the minimum reliable workflow on EFI partition (do NOT create a new one if Windows already has it) and what to verify before moving on.

slides.narration_seed: Define Bootloader location (important) in plain language, then connect the term to one practical robotics consequence so students do not memorize it in isolation.
must_cover: At the bottom: Device for boot loader installation
Choose the main disk (example: /dev/nvme0n1 or /dev/sda)
Do NOT choose a specific partition like /dev/nvme0n1p5
Then click Install Now
